short_excerpt: Preserving and Resetting State
text: Preserving and Resetting State

short_excerpt: State is isolated between components. React keeps track of which state belongs to which component based on their place in the UI tree. You can control when to preserve state and when to reset it between re-renders.
text: State is isolated between components. React keeps track of which state belongs to which component based on their place in the UI tree. You can control when to preserve state and when to reset it between re-renders.

short_excerpt: You will learn
text: You will learn

short_excerpt: When React chooses to preserve or reset the state
text: When React chooses to preserve or reset the state

short_excerpt: How to force React to reset component’s state
text: How to force React to reset component’s state

short_excerpt: How keys and types affect whether the state is preserved
text: How keys and types affect whether the state is preserved

short_excerpt: State is tied to a position in the render tree
text: State is tied to a position in the render tree

short_excerpt: React builds render trees for the component structure in your UI.
text: React builds render trees for the component structure in your UI.

short_excerpt: When you give a component state, you might think the state “lives” inside the component. But the state is actually held inside React. React associates each piece of state it’s holding with the correct component by where that component sits in the render tree.
text: When you give a component state, you might think the state “lives” inside the component. But the state is actually held inside React. React associates each piece of state it’s holding with the correct component by where that component sits in the render tree.

short_excerpt: Here, there is only one <Counter /> JSX tag, but it’s rendered at two different positions:
text: Here, there is only one <Counter /> JSX tag, but it’s rendered at two different positions:

text: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function App ( ) { const counter = < Counter /> ; return ( < div > { counter } { counter } </ div > ) ; } function Counter ( ) { const [ score , setScore ] = useState ( 0 ) ; const [ hover , setHover ] = useState ( false ) ; let className = 'counter' ; if ( hover ) { className += ' hover' ; } return ( < div className = { className } onPointerEnter = { ( ) => setHover ( true ) } onPointerLeave = { ( ) => setHover ( false ) } > < h1 > { score } </ h1 > < button onClick = { ( ) => setScore ( score + 1 ) } > Add one </ button > </ div > ) ; }

short_excerpt: Here’s how these look as a tree:
text: Here’s how these look as a tree:

short_excerpt: React tree
text: React tree

short_excerpt: These are two separate counters because each is rendered at its own position in the tree. You don’t usually have to think about these positions to use React, but it can be useful to understand how it works.
text: These are two separate counters because each is rendered at its own position in the tree. You don’t usually have to think about these positions to use React, but it can be useful to understand how it works.

short_excerpt: In React, each component on the screen has fully isolated state. For example, if you render two Counter components side by side, each of them will get its own, independent, score and hover states.
text: In React, each component on the screen has fully isolated state. For example, if you render two Counter components side by side, each of them will get its own, independent, score and hover states.

short_excerpt: Try clicking both counters and notice they don’t affect each other:
text: Try clicking both counters and notice they don’t affect each other:

short_excerpt: As you can see, when one counter is updated, only the state for that component is updated:
text: As you can see, when one counter is updated, only the state for that component is updated:

text: Updating state

short_excerpt: React will keep the state around for as long as you render the same component at the same position in the tree. To see this, increment both counters, then remove the second component by unchecking “Render the second counter” checkbox, and then add it back by ticking it again:
text: React will keep the state around for as long as you render the same component at the same position in the tree. To see this, increment both counters, then remove the second component by unchecking “Render the second counter” checkbox, and then add it back by ticking it again:

short_excerpt: Notice how the moment you stop rendering the second counter, its state disappears completely. That’s because when React removes a component, it destroys its state.
text: Notice how the moment you stop rendering the second counter, its state disappears completely. That’s because when React removes a component, it destroys its state.

short_excerpt: Deleting a component
text: Deleting a component

short_excerpt: When you tick “Render the second counter”, a second Counter and its state are initialized from scratch ( score = 0 ) and added to the DOM.
text: When you tick “Render the second counter”, a second Counter and its state are initialized from scratch ( score = 0 ) and added to the DOM.

short_excerpt: Adding a component
text: Adding a component

short_excerpt: React preserves a component’s state for as long as it’s being rendered at its position in the UI tree. If it gets removed, or a different component gets rendered at the same position, React discards its state.
text: React preserves a component’s state for as long as it’s being rendered at its position in the UI tree. If it gets removed, or a different component gets rendered at the same position, React discards its state.

short_excerpt: Same component at the same position preserves state
text: Same component at the same position preserves state

short_excerpt: In this example, there are two different <Counter /> tags:
text: In this example, there are two different <Counter /> tags:

short_excerpt: When you tick or clear the checkbox, the counter state does not get reset. Whether isFancy is true or false , you always have a <Counter /> as the first child of the div returned from the root App component:
text: When you tick or clear the checkbox, the counter state does not get reset. Whether isFancy is true or false , you always have a <Counter /> as the first child of the div returned from the root App component:

short_excerpt: Updating the App state does not reset the Counter because Counter stays in the same position
text: Updating the App state does not reset the Counter because Counter stays in the same position

short_excerpt: It’s the same component at the same position, so from React’s perspective, it’s the same counter.
text: It’s the same component at the same position, so from React’s perspective, it’s the same counter.

short_excerpt: Pitfall
text: Pitfall

short_excerpt: Remember that it’s the position in the UI tree—not in the JSX markup—that matters to React! This component has two return clauses with different <Counter /> JSX tags inside and outside the if :
text: Remember that it’s the position in the UI tree—not in the JSX markup—that matters to React! This component has two return clauses with different <Counter /> JSX tags inside and outside the if :

short_excerpt: You might expect the state to reset when you tick checkbox, but it doesn’t! This is because both of these <Counter /> tags are rendered at the same position. React doesn’t know where you place the conditions in your function. All it “sees” is the tree you return.
text: You might expect the state to reset when you tick checkbox, but it doesn’t! This is because both of these <Counter /> tags are rendered at the same position. React doesn’t know where you place the conditions in your function. All it “sees” is the tree you return.

short_excerpt: <Counter />
text: <Counter />

short_excerpt: In both cases, the App component returns a <div> with <Counter /> as a first child. To React, these two counters have the same “address”: the first child of the first child of the root. This is how React matches them up between the previous and next renders, regardless of how you structure your logic.
text: In both cases, the App component returns a <div> with <Counter /> as a first child. To React, these two counters have the same “address”: the first child of the first child of the root. This is how React matches them up between the previous and next renders, regardless of how you structure your logic.

short_excerpt: Different components at the same position reset state
text: Different components at the same position reset state

short_excerpt: Here, you switch between different component types at the same position. Initially, the first child of the <div> contained a Counter . But when you swapped in a p , React removed the Counter from the UI tree and destroyed its state.
text: Here, you switch between different component types at the same position. Initially, the first child of the <div> contained a Counter . But when you swapped in a p , React removed the Counter from the UI tree and destroyed its state.

short_excerpt: When Counter changes to p , the Counter is deleted and the p is added
text: When Counter changes to p , the Counter is deleted and the p is added

short_excerpt: When switching back, the p is deleted and the Counter is added
text: When switching back, the p is deleted and the Counter is added

short_excerpt: Also, when you render a different component in the same position, it resets the state of its entire subtree. To see how this works, increment the counter and then tick the checkbox:
text: Also, when you render a different component in the same position, it resets the state of its entire subtree. To see how this works, increment the counter and then tick the checkbox:

( < div > < Counter isFancy = { true } /> </ div > ) : ( < section > < Counter isFancy = { false } /> </ section > ) } < label > < input type = "checkbox" checked = { isFancy } onChange = { e => { setIsFancy ( e . target . checked ) } } /> Use fancy styling </ label > </ div > ) ; } function Counter ( { isFancy } ) { const [ score , setScore ] = useState ( 0 ) ; const [ hover , setHover ] = useState ( false ) ; let className = 'counter' ; if (

short_excerpt: The counter state gets reset when you click the checkbox. Although you render a Counter , the first child of the div changes from a section to a div . When the child section was removed from the DOM, the whole tree below it (including the Counter and its state) was destroyed as well.
text: The counter state gets reset when you click the checkbox. Although you render a Counter , the first child of the div changes from a section to a div . When the child section was removed from the DOM, the whole tree below it (including the Counter and its state) was destroyed as well.

short_excerpt: When section changes to div , the section is deleted and the new div is added
text: When section changes to div , the section is deleted and the new div is added

short_excerpt: When switching back, the div is deleted and the new section is added
text: When switching back, the div is deleted and the new section is added

short_excerpt: As a rule of thumb, if you want to preserve the state between re-renders, the structure of your tree needs to “match up” from one render to another. If the structure is different, the state gets destroyed because React destroys state when it removes a component from the tree.
text: As a rule of thumb, if you want to preserve the state between re-renders, the structure of your tree needs to “match up” from one render to another. If the structure is different, the state gets destroyed because React destroys state when it removes a component from the tree.

short_excerpt: This is why you should not nest component function definitions.
text: This is why you should not nest component function definitions.

short_excerpt: Here, the MyTextField component function is defined inside MyComponent :
text: Here, the MyTextField component function is defined inside MyComponent :

short_excerpt: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function MyComponent ( ) { const [ counter , setCounter ] = useState ( 0 ) ; function MyTextField ( ) { const [ text , setText ] = useState ( '' ) ; return ( < input value = { text } onChange = { e => setText ( e . target . value ) } /> ) ; } return ( < > < MyTextField /> < button onClick = { ( ) => { setCounter ( counter + 1 ) } } > Clicked { counter } times </ button > </ > ) ; }
text: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function MyComponent ( ) { const [ counter , setCounter ] = useState ( 0 ) ; function MyTextField ( ) { const [ text , setText ] = useState ( '' ) ; return ( < input value = { text } onChange = { e => setText ( e . target . value ) } /> ) ; } return ( < > < MyTextField /> < button onClick = { ( ) => { setCounter ( counter + 1 ) } } > Clicked { counter } times </ button > </ > ) ; }

short_excerpt: Every time you click the button, the input state disappears! This is because a different MyTextField function is created for every render of MyComponent . You’re rendering a different component in the same position, so React resets all state below. This leads to bugs and performance problems. To avoid this problem, always declare component functions at the top level, and don’t nest their definitions.
text: Every time you click the button, the input state disappears! This is because a different MyTextField function is created for every render of MyComponent . You’re rendering a different component in the same position, so React resets all state below. This leads to bugs and performance problems. To avoid this problem, always declare component functions at the top level, and don’t nest their definitions.

short_excerpt: Resetting state at the same position
text: Resetting state at the same position

short_excerpt: By default, React preserves state of a component while it stays at the same position. Usually, this is exactly what you want, so it makes sense as the default behavior. But sometimes, you may want to reset a component’s state. Consider this app that lets two players keep track of their scores during each turn:
text: By default, React preserves state of a component while it stays at the same position. Usually, this is exactly what you want, so it makes sense as the default behavior. But sometimes, you may want to reset a component’s state. Consider this app that lets two players keep track of their scores during each turn:

short_excerpt: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function Scoreboard ( ) { const [ isPlayerA , setIsPlayerA ] = useState ( true ) ; return ( < div > { isPlayerA ? ( < Counter person = "Taylor" /> ) : ( < Counter person = "Sarah" /> ) } < button onClick = { ( ) => { setIsPlayerA ( ! isPlayerA ) ; } } > Next player! </ button > </ div > ) ; } function Counter ( { person } ) { const [ score , setScore ] = useState ( 0 ) ; const [ hover , setHover ] = useState ( false ) ; let className = 'counter' ; if ( hover ) { className += ' hover' ; } return ( < div className = { className } onPointerEnter =
text: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function Scoreboard ( ) { const [ isPlayerA , setIsPlayerA ] = useState ( true ) ; return ( < div > { isPlayerA ? ( < Counter person = "Taylor" /> ) : ( < Counter person = "Sarah" /> ) } < button onClick = { ( ) => { setIsPlayerA ( ! isPlayerA ) ; } } > Next player! </ button > </ div > ) ; } function Counter ( { person } ) { const [ score , setScore ] = useState ( 0 ) ; const [ hover , setHover ] = useState ( false ) ; let className = 'counter' ; if ( hover ) { className += ' hover' ; } return ( < div className = { className } onPointerEnter = { ( ) => setHover ( true ) } onPointerLeave = { ( ) => setHover ( false ) } > < h1 > { person } 's score: { score } </ h1 > < button onClick = { ( ) => setScore ( score + 1 ) } > Add one </ button > </ div > ) ; }

short_excerpt: Currently, when you change the player, the score is preserved. The two Counter s appear in the same position, so React sees them as the same Counter whose person prop has changed.
text: Currently, when you change the player, the score is preserved. The two Counter s appear in the same position, so React sees them as the same Counter whose person prop has changed.

short_excerpt: But conceptually, in this app they should be two separate counters. They might appear in the same place in the UI, but one is a counter for Taylor, and another is a counter for Sarah.
text: But conceptually, in this app they should be two separate counters. They might appear in the same place in the UI, but one is a counter for Taylor, and another is a counter for Sarah.

short_excerpt: There are two ways to reset state when switching between them:
text: There are two ways to reset state when switching between them:

short_excerpt: Render components in different positions
text: Render components in different positions

short_excerpt: Give each component an explicit identity with key
text: Give each component an explicit identity with key

short_excerpt: Option 1: Rendering a component in different positions
text: Option 1: Rendering a component in different positions

short_excerpt: If you want these two Counter s to be independent, you can render them in two different positions:
text: If you want these two Counter s to be independent, you can render them in two different positions:

text: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function Scoreboard ( ) { const [ isPlayerA , setIsPlayerA ] = useState ( true ) ; return ( < div > { isPlayerA && < Counter person = "Taylor" /> } { ! isPlayerA && < Counter person = "Sarah" /> } < button onClick = { ( ) => { setIsPlayerA ( ! isPlayerA ) ; } } > Next player! </ button > </ div > ) ; } function Counter ( { person } ) { const [ score , setScore ] = useState ( 0 ) ; const [ hover , setHover ] = useState ( false ) ; let className = 'counter' ; if ( hover ) { className += ' hover' ; } return ( < div className = { className } onPointerEnter = { ( ) => setHover ( true ) } onPointerLeave = { ( ) => setHover ( false ) } > < h1 > { person } 's score: { score } </ h1 > < button onClick = { ( ) => setScore ( score + 1 ) } > Add one </ button > </ div > ) ; }

short_excerpt: Initially, isPlayerA is true . So the first position contains Counter state, and the second one is empty.
text: Initially, isPlayerA is true . So the first position contains Counter state, and the second one is empty.

short_excerpt: When you click the “Next player” button the first position clears but the second one now contains a Counter .
text: When you click the “Next player” button the first position clears but the second one now contains a Counter .

text: Initial state

text: Clicking “next”

short_excerpt: Each Counter ’s state gets destroyed each time it’s removed from the DOM. This is why they reset every time you click the button.
text: Each Counter ’s state gets destroyed each time it’s removed from the DOM. This is why they reset every time you click the button.

short_excerpt: This solution is convenient when you only have a few independent components rendered in the same place. In this example, you only have two, so it’s not a hassle to render both separately in the JSX.
text: This solution is convenient when you only have a few independent components rendered in the same place. In this example, you only have two, so it’s not a hassle to render both separately in the JSX.

short_excerpt: Option 2: Resetting state with a key
text: Option 2: Resetting state with a key

short_excerpt: There is also another, more generic, way to reset a component’s state.
text: There is also another, more generic, way to reset a component’s state.

short_excerpt: You might have seen key s when rendering lists. Keys aren’t just for lists! You can use keys to make React distinguish between any components. By default, React uses order within the parent (“first counter”, “second counter”) to discern between components. But keys let you tell React that this is not just a first counter, or a second counter, but a specific counter—for example, Taylor’s counter. This way, React will know Taylor’s counter wherever it appears in the tree!
text: You might have seen key s when rendering lists. Keys aren’t just for lists! You can use keys to make React distinguish between any components. By default, React uses order within the parent (“first counter”, “second counter”) to discern between components. But keys let you tell React that this is not just a first counter, or a second counter, but a specific counter—for example, Taylor’s counter. This way, React will know Taylor’s counter wherever it appears in the tree!

short_excerpt: In this example, the two <Counter /> s don’t share state even though they appear in the same place in JSX:
text: In this example, the two <Counter /> s don’t share state even though they appear in the same place in JSX:

short_excerpt: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function Scoreboard ( ) { const [ isPlayerA , setIsPlayerA ] = useState ( true ) ; return ( < div > { isPlayerA ? ( < Counter key = "Taylor" person = "Taylor" /> ) : ( < Counter key = "Sarah" person = "Sarah" /> ) } < button onClick = { ( ) => { setIsPlayerA ( ! isPlayerA ) ; } } > Next player! </ button > </ div > ) ; } function Counter ( { person } ) { const [ score , setScore ] = useState ( 0 ) ; const [ hover , setHover ] = useState ( false ) ; let className = 'counter' ; if ( hover ) { className += ' hover' ; } return ( < div className = {
text: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function Scoreboard ( ) { const [ isPlayerA , setIsPlayerA ] = useState ( true ) ; return ( < div > { isPlayerA ? ( < Counter key = "Taylor" person = "Taylor" /> ) : ( < Counter key = "Sarah" person = "Sarah" /> ) } < button onClick = { ( ) => { setIsPlayerA ( ! isPlayerA ) ; } } > Next player! </ button > </ div > ) ; } function Counter ( { person } ) { const [ score , setScore ] = useState ( 0 ) ; const [ hover , setHover ] = useState ( false ) ; let className = 'counter' ; if ( hover ) { className += ' hover' ; } return ( < div className = { className } onPointerEnter = { ( ) => setHover ( true ) } onPointerLeave = { ( ) => setHover ( false ) } > < h1 > { person } 's score: { score } </ h1 > < button onClick = { ( ) => setScore ( score + 1 ) } > Add one </ button > </ div > ) ; }

short_excerpt: Switching between Taylor and Sarah does not preserve the state. This is because you gave them different key s:
text: Switching between Taylor and Sarah does not preserve the state. This is because you gave them different key s:

short_excerpt: key
text: key

short_excerpt: { isPlayerA ? ( < Counter key = "Taylor" person = "Taylor" /> ) : ( < Counter key = "Sarah" person = "Sarah" /> ) }
text: { isPlayerA ? ( < Counter key = "Taylor" person = "Taylor" /> ) : ( < Counter key = "Sarah" person = "Sarah" /> ) }

short_excerpt: Specifying a key tells React to use the key itself as part of the position, instead of their order within the parent. This is why, even though you render them in the same place in JSX, React sees them as two different counters, and so they will never share state. Every time a counter appears on the screen, its state is created. Every time it is removed, its state is destroyed. Toggling between them resets their state over and over.
text: Specifying a key tells React to use the key itself as part of the position, instead of their order within the parent. This is why, even though you render them in the same place in JSX, React sees them as two different counters, and so they will never share state. Every time a counter appears on the screen, its state is created. Every time it is removed, its state is destroyed. Toggling between them resets their state over and over.

short_excerpt: Note
text: Note

short_excerpt: Remember that keys are not globally unique. They only specify the position within the parent .
text: Remember that keys are not globally unique. They only specify the position within the parent .

short_excerpt: Resetting a form with a key
text: Resetting a form with a key

short_excerpt: Resetting state with a key is particularly useful when dealing with forms.
text: Resetting state with a key is particularly useful when dealing with forms.

short_excerpt: In this chat app, the <Chat> component contains the text input state:
text: In this chat app, the <Chat> component contains the text input state:

short_excerpt: import { useState } from 'react' ; import Chat from './Chat.js' ; import ContactList from './ContactList.js' ; export default function Messenger ( ) { const [ to , setTo ] = useState ( contacts [ 0 ] ) ; return ( < div > < ContactList contacts = { contacts } selectedContact = { to } onSelect = { contact => setTo ( contact ) } /> < Chat contact = { to } /> </ div > ) } const contacts = [ { id : 0 , name : 'Taylor' , email : 'taylor@mail.com' } , { id : 1 , name : 'Alice' , email : 'alice@mail.com' } , { id : 2 , name : 'Bob' , email : 'bob@mail.com' } ] ;
text: import { useState } from 'react' ; import Chat from './Chat.js' ; import ContactList from './ContactList.js' ; export default function Messenger ( ) { const [ to , setTo ] = useState ( contacts [ 0 ] ) ; return ( < div > < ContactList contacts = { contacts } selectedContact = { to } onSelect = { contact => setTo ( contact ) } /> < Chat contact = { to } /> </ div > ) } const contacts = [ { id : 0 , name : 'Taylor' , email : 'taylor@mail.com' } , { id : 1 , name : 'Alice' , email : 'alice@mail.com' } , { id : 2 , name : 'Bob' , email : 'bob@mail.com' } ] ;

short_excerpt: Try entering something into the input, and then press “Alice” or “Bob” to choose a different recipient. You will notice that the input state is preserved because the <Chat> is rendered at the same position in the tree.
text: Try entering something into the input, and then press “Alice” or “Bob” to choose a different recipient. You will notice that the input state is preserved because the <Chat> is rendered at the same position in the tree.

short_excerpt: In many apps, this may be the desired behavior, but not in a chat app! You don’t want to let the user send a message they already typed to a wrong person due to an accidental click. To fix it, add a key :
text: In many apps, this may be the desired behavior, but not in a chat app! You don’t want to let the user send a message they already typed to a wrong person due to an accidental click. To fix it, add a key :

short_excerpt: < Chat key = { to . id } contact = { to } />
text: < Chat key = { to . id } contact = { to } />

short_excerpt: This ensures that when you select a different recipient, the Chat component will be recreated from scratch, including any state in the tree below it. React will also re-create the DOM elements instead of reusing them.
text: This ensures that when you select a different recipient, the Chat component will be recreated from scratch, including any state in the tree below it. React will also re-create the DOM elements instead of reusing them.

short_excerpt: Now switching the recipient always clears the text field:
text: Now switching the recipient always clears the text field:

short_excerpt: import { useState } from 'react' ; import Chat from './Chat.js' ; import ContactList from './ContactList.js' ; export default function Messenger ( ) { const [ to , setTo ] = useState ( contacts [ 0 ] ) ; return ( < div > < ContactList contacts = { contacts } selectedContact = { to } onSelect = { contact => setTo ( contact ) } /> < Chat key = { to . id } contact = { to } /> </ div > ) } const contacts = [ { id : 0 , name : 'Taylor' , email : 'taylor@mail.com' } , { id : 1 , name : 'Alice' , email : 'alice@mail.com' } , { id : 2 , name : 'Bob' , email : 'bob@mail.com' } ] ;
text: import { useState } from 'react' ; import Chat from './Chat.js' ; import ContactList from './ContactList.js' ; export default function Messenger ( ) { const [ to , setTo ] = useState ( contacts [ 0 ] ) ; return ( < div > < ContactList contacts = { contacts } selectedContact = { to } onSelect = { contact => setTo ( contact ) } /> < Chat key = { to . id } contact = { to } /> </ div > ) } const contacts = [ { id : 0 , name : 'Taylor' , email : 'taylor@mail.com' } , { id : 1 , name : 'Alice' , email : 'alice@mail.com' } , { id : 2 , name : 'Bob' , email : 'bob@mail.com' } ] ;

short_excerpt: In a real chat app, you’d probably want to recover the input state when the user selects the previous recipient again. There are a few ways to keep the state “alive” for a component that’s no longer visible:
text: In a real chat app, you’d probably want to recover the input state when the user selects the previous recipient again. There are a few ways to keep the state “alive” for a component that’s no longer visible:

short_excerpt: You could render all chats instead of just the current one, but hide all the others with CSS. The chats would not get removed from the tree, so their local state would be preserved. This solution works great for simple UIs. But it can get very slow if the hidden trees are large and contain a lot of DOM nodes.
text: You could render all chats instead of just the current one, but hide all the others with CSS. The chats would not get removed from the tree, so their local state would be preserved. This solution works great for simple UIs. But it can get very slow if the hidden trees are large and contain a lot of DOM nodes.

short_excerpt: You could lift the state up and hold the pending message for each recipient in the parent component. This way, when the child components get removed, it doesn’t matter, because it’s the parent that keeps the important information. This is the most common solution.
text: You could lift the state up and hold the pending message for each recipient in the parent component. This way, when the child components get removed, it doesn’t matter, because it’s the parent that keeps the important information. This is the most common solution.

short_excerpt: You might also use a different source in addition to React state. For example, you probably want a message draft to persist even if the user accidentally closes the page. To implement this, you could have the Chat component initialize its state by reading from the localStorage , and save the drafts there too.
text: You might also use a different source in addition to React state. For example, you probably want a message draft to persist even if the user accidentally closes the page. To implement this, you could have the Chat component initialize its state by reading from the localStorage , and save the drafts there too.

short_excerpt: localStorage
text: localStorage

short_excerpt: No matter which strategy you pick, a chat with Alice is conceptually distinct from a chat with Bob , so it makes sense to give a key to the <Chat> tree based on the current recipient.
text: No matter which strategy you pick, a chat with Alice is conceptually distinct from a chat with Bob , so it makes sense to give a key to the <Chat> tree based on the current recipient.

short_excerpt: Recap
text: Recap

short_excerpt: React keeps state for as long as the same component is rendered at the same position.
text: React keeps state for as long as the same component is rendered at the same position.

short_excerpt: State is not kept in JSX tags. It’s associated with the tree position in which you put that JSX.
text: State is not kept in JSX tags. It’s associated with the tree position in which you put that JSX.

short_excerpt: You can force a subtree to reset its state by giving it a different key.
text: You can force a subtree to reset its state by giving it a different key.

short_excerpt: Don’t nest component definitions, or you’ll reset state by accident.
text: Don’t nest component definitions, or you’ll reset state by accident.

short_excerpt: Try out some challenges
text: Try out some challenges

short_excerpt: This example shows a message when you press the button. However, pressing the button also accidentally resets the input. Why does this happen? Fix it so that pressing the button does not reset the input text.
text: This example shows a message when you press the button. However, pressing the button also accidentally resets the input. Why does this happen? Fix it so that pressing the button does not reset the input text.

short_excerpt: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function App ( ) { const [ showHint , setShowHint ] = useState ( false ) ; if ( showHint ) { return ( < div > < p > < i > Hint: Your favorite city? </ i > </ p > < Form /> < button onClick = { ( ) => { setShowHint ( false ) ; } } > Hide hint </ button > </ div > ) ; } return ( < div > < Form /> < button onClick = { ( ) => { setShowHint ( true ) ; } } > Show hint </ button > </ div > ) ; } function Form ( ) { const [ text , setText ] = useState ( '' ) ; return ( < textarea value = { text } onChange = { e => setText ( e . target . value ) } /> )
text: import { useState } from 'react' ; export default function App ( ) { const [ showHint , setShowHint ] = useState ( false ) ; if ( showHint ) { return ( < div > < p > < i > Hint: Your favorite city? </ i > </ p > < Form /> < button onClick = { ( ) => { setShowHint ( false ) ; } } > Hide hint </ button > </ div > ) ; } return ( < div > < Form /> < button onClick = { ( ) => { setShowHint ( true ) ; } } > Show hint </ button > </ div > ) ; } function Form ( ) { const [ text , setText ] = useState ( '' ) ; return ( < textarea value = { text } onChange = { e => setText ( e . target . value ) } /> ) ; }
